content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Let me start with what I’m not claiming. Opossum is a good library. It’s the de-facto circuit breaker for Node.js, it’s maintained by the Nodeshift team, it’s battle-tested, and if you’re running a single instance of a service, you should probably use it instead of mine. Cockatiel is similarly solid if you want the Polly-style policy composition.
I built redeye-breaker anyway, because both of them share one architectural assumption that quietly breaks in production: circuit breaker state lives in process memory.
That assumption is fine right up until you run more than one instance of your service. Then it isn’t. Here are three concrete scenarios where per-process state isn’t just a weaker version of the right answer — it’s structurally the wrong shape for the problem.
Use case one: a fleet of workers hitting a rate-limited API
Say you’re running twenty workers against an LLM API with a shared organization-level quota. Worker 7 gets a 429. Its local breaker trips. Good.
Workers 1 through 6 and 8 through 20 have no idea this happened. Each of them will keep firing requests until it personally accumulates enough failures to trip its own breaker. With opossum’s default error threshold, that’s potentially hundreds of doomed requests across the fleet — every one of them burning retry budget, extending the rate-limit penalty window, and in some pricing models, costing actual money.
The quota is shared. The failure signal should be too. In redeye-breaker’s distributed mode, when one worker’s breaker opens, the state propagates through Redis and the whole fleet backs off together. One 429 becomes one fleet-wide cooldown instead of twenty independent discoveries of the same fact.
You can approximate this with opossum by building your own Redis pub/sub layer on top of its events. I know because that’s roughly where redeye-breaker started, and I’d gently suggest you don’t. The coordination logic is where all the hard bugs live — I’ll come back to that.
Use case two: horizontally scaled services behind a load balancer
This is the same problem wearing a different costume, but the failure mode is worse because your users see it.
You have twelve pods behind a load balancer, all calling the same downstream payment service. The payment service starts failing. With per-process breakers, each pod independently walks the same path: accumulate failures, trip, cool down, half-open, probe. Your users’ requests are landing on pods at random, which means some fraction of traffic keeps hitting pods whose breakers haven’t tripped yet — and those requests hang until timeout instead of failing fast.
The math is unflattering. If your breaker needs ten failures to trip, twelve pods need roughly 120 failed user requests before the fleet has fully reacted. A shared breaker needs ten. Everything after that fails fast with a fallback instead of a thirty-second timeout.
There’s a second-order benefit on recovery, too. With independent breakers, twelve pods all enter half-open around the same time and send twelve probe requests at a service that’s just barely recovering. That’s a small thundering herd aimed at your most fragile dependency at its most fragile moment. Coordinated state means coordinated, controlled probing.
Use case three: autoscaling and ephemeral instances
Opossum’s documentation actually acknowledges this one — it supports manually initializing breaker state, with serverless and container platforms as the stated use case. The workaround exists because the problem is real: every new instance starts with a closed breaker and zero knowledge.
Picture the worst version. Your downstream dependency fails, latency spikes, your autoscaler sees the load and spins up new pods to help. Every new pod arrives with a fresh, closed breaker and immediately starts hammering the exact dependency that’s already down. Your scaling response is actively making the outage worse, and it will keep doing so until each new pod independently learns what every existing pod already knew.
With shared state, a new instance inherits the fleet’s knowledge at startup. The breaker is already open. The pod serves fallbacks from its first request. Scaling up during an incident stops being an accidental attack on your own infrastructure.
What teams actually do about this today
None of these problems are new, and teams have been coping with them for years. It’s worth being honest about the existing answers, because they’re the real alternatives to redeye-breaker — and some of them might be right for you.
The most common strategy is to simply live with it. Each instance protects itself, the fleet converges on “tripped” eventually, and for pure failure detection this is often tolerable. It’s wasteful, not catastrophic. If your downstream calls are cheap and your instance count is small, the waste may never be worth engineering away.
The second strategy pushes circuit breaking out of the application entirely, into infrastructure. Service meshes like Istio do outlier detection at the Envoy proxy layer, and API gateways offer breaker plugins. Because the proxy sees traffic across all pods, you get fleet-wide behavior for free. The cost is coarseness: the mesh understands status codes and timeouts, not your application’s semantics. A 200 response whose body says “quota exhausted” is invisible to it. If your failure signal lives in application logic, you can’t fully delegate to this layer.
The third strategy is the do-it-yourself Redis layer. Opossum emits events on every state transition, so teams wire those events into pub/sub and force remote breakers open by hand. I have particular feelings about this one, because it’s roughly where redeye-breaker started — and the coordination code is precisely where the hard bugs live. Lock ownership, in-flight races, reconciliation after a partition: most homegrown versions have these bugs and don’t know it, because the code path only executes during incidents, which is the worst possible time to discover your resilience layer isn’t resilient.
The fourth, specific to serverless: opossum supports serializing breaker state and re-initializing new instances from it, so Lambda-heavy teams persist state to an external store between invocations. It’s officially supported, but the read/write logic and its race conditions are yours to own.
And fifth, some teams sidestep breakers for the shared-quota case entirely and coordinate the fleet’s request budget with a distributed rate limiter, keeping breakers local and failure-only. That’s a legitimate design — it just means running two coordination systems for what is arguably one problem.
redeye-breaker is essentially strategy three, done properly and maintained as a library instead of rediscovered as an incident. If one of the other four fits your constraints better, use it.
The part where I tell you the tradeoffs
Distributed state is not free, and I’d rather you hear the costs from me than discover them in an incident review.
First, you’re adding Redis as a dependency, which means Redis is now a thing that can fail. This is the problem I spent most of v0.5.x on. redeye-breaker uses a hybrid local cache so that when Redis is unreachable, breakers degrade to local-only operation instead of failing open or blocking on a dead connection. Getting that degradation path right surfaced some genuinely nasty bugs — a cache death spiral, an in-flight read race, a reconciler that violated its own contract. They’re fixed, but I mention them because “just share state through Redis” sounds simple and is not.
Second, coordination has latency. State propagation is fast but not instantaneous, so there’s a small window where instances disagree. For circuit breaking this is acceptable — a breaker that trips fleet-wide in tens of milliseconds is still enormously better than twelve breakers tripping independently over several seconds — but if you need strict consensus semantics, this is not that, and I won’t pretend otherwise.
Third, redeye-breaker is at v0.5.2. Opossum has been in production at serious scale since 2016. If your workload is a single instance, or your instances genuinely don’t share fate downstream, the honest recommendation is to use the boring, proven thing.
The actual decision rule
It comes down to one question: do your instances share fate with respect to the dependency?
A shared quota, a shared downstream service, a shared rate limit — if one instance learning “this is broken” is information the others need, then per-process breakers are making each instance relearn the same lesson at your users’ expense. That’s the problem redeye-breaker exists to solve.
If your instances don’t share fate, you don’t need this, and adding Redis to avoid a problem you don’t have is how you acquire problems you didn’t have.
redeye-breaker is open source and on npm. The distributed-mode internals — the ownership tokens, the compare-and-delete Lua scripts, the reconciliation logic — are all in the repo if you want to see how the coordination actually works. Issues and war stories welcome.
